decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate property represents a general recycled content value, whereas our term specifies the share of cobalt pre‑consumer recycled material.
decisions.qaRationale: Our term is a specific specialization (single material cobalt, single pre-consumer stream) of the upstream generic recycled-content measure, so it is properly narrower than the candidate.

decisions.bulkRationale: The GS1 property refers to general sustainability and recycling information, whereas our term specifies the quantitative carbon‑footprint contribution from the recycling phase.
decisions.qaRationale: Our term is a single quantitative carbon-footprint metric for the recycling phase, far narrower than GS1's general link to sustainability and recycling information, so it is at most a broadMatch (NARROW) to the GS1 property.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both terms refer to the capacity threshold at which a battery is considered exhausted or reaches end of life.
decisions.qaRationale: Both properties denote the end-of-life capacity threshold of a battery, but the undefined upstream term may express an absolute capacity value rather than our fade percentage, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both refer to energy throughput, but the upstream term lacks the explicit lifetime scope present in our definition.
decisions.qaRationale: Our term is a specific lifetime-scoped energy throughput measure that overlaps with the undefined upstream energyThroughput but cannot be confirmed interchangeable given the missing upstream definition, so a weaker CLOSE is warranted.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate property refers to a self‑discharge current, whereas the OUR term specifies a percentage rate per month.
decisions.qaRationale: Both properties characterize a battery's self-discharge phenomenon, but ours quantifies it as a percentage rate per month while the upstream term frames it as a current, so they overlap conceptually without being interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both terms refer to the rate at which a battery loses charge over time, though the candidate term lacks explicit unit or monthly percentage specification.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are battery self-discharge properties, but 'evolution' connotes a change-over-time trajectory rather than our single instantaneous percent-per-month rate, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate property refers to any internet resource, while the OUR term is specifically a URL pointing to a document.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties holding a URL to a referenced resource, but ours is constrained to a document on a DismantlingDocument while gs1:referencedFileURL covers any internet resource, making ours the narrower term.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate property refers to the carbon footprint of production activities, which is a subset of the total absolute carbon footprint represented by our term.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are carbon-footprint properties, and the candidate's label scopes it to the production stage while our term is the total (absolute, full-lifecycle) footprint, making ours the broader concept—though the candidate's missing definition leaves residual uncertainty.

decisions.bulkRationale: The schema.org property represents a generic product model identifier, while our term is a battery‑specific identifier required by regulation.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing a product's model identifier; schema:model is the generic product-model property and our term is a battery-specific, regulation-mandated narrowing of it.

decisions.bulkRationale: The GS1 property is a generic URL to any internet resource, while our term specifically denotes a URL pointing to a measurement certificate document.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties pointing to a resource URL, and our measurement-certificate URL is a specific specialization of GS1's generic referenced-file URL, so ours is narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both refer to safety information for the product, but our term specifically points to a general instructions document (often end‑of‑life), whereas the candidate covers broader consumer safety content.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are product safety-information properties, but ours is a URL to an end-of-life-specific safety instructions document while the GS1 term covers general consumer-use safety information, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties refer to the meaning or content of a label, but the candidate lacks a definition and may be broader.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are label-content properties, but the upstream term lacks any definition and 'meaning' (semantic interpretation) is not clearly interchangeable with 'subject matter', so only an approximate overlap can be asserted.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both describe a role of an entity, but the candidate is a general organization role while our property specifically refers to economic operators.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties assigning a role to an entity, but ours is restricted to economic-operator roles (a subset of the candidate's general party/organization roles), making our term narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both terms are the same property name and intended to capture the role of an economic operator, indicating identical concept.
decisions.qaRationale: Our eubat:operatorRole is a property capturing the economic operator's role and is built directly on oec: types (domain oec:OperatorInformation, range oec:OperatorRole), so it is interchangeable with the oec core operatorRole property it anchors to.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both describe an agent’s function, but the candidate is a general role within any participation, whereas our property specifically denotes the economic operator’s role.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties denoting an actor's function; m8g:role is the generic role of any Agent within a Participation while our property is the constrained case of an economic operator's role, so ours is narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property specifies a supplier’s contact point, whereas the upstream term describes any main contact point of a resource; thus our concept is a narrower specialization.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties linking an entity to its contact information, but ours is restricted to a supplier organization's contact point, making it a specialization of the upstream generic contactPoint.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties link an organization to a contact point, but the candidate is a generic contactPoints property whereas our term specifically denotes a supplier’s contact.
decisions.qaRationale: Both link an organization to a contact point, but our supplierContact is a role-specific subset of schema.org's generic contactPoints, making ours narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: eubat:expectedLifetime covers both months and years, whereas dppk:expectedLifetimeYears is limited to years only.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties for manufacturer-declared expected service life; ours accepts a QuantitativeValue in months or years and thus subsumes the year-only upstream property, making ours broader.

decisions.bulkRationale: The OUR property specifies the percentage of recycled cobalt, which is a more specific aspect of the generic recycled content concept represented by the candidate.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties; ours measures recycled fraction for a single material (cobalt), a material-specific specialization of the generic recycledContent property, so OUR term is narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both terms refer to the amount of power that remains available, but our property explicitly ties it to various state‑of‑charge levels whereas the candidate term is a more generic remaining power concept.
decisions.qaRationale: Both describe remaining/current power capability of a battery, but ours is a structured measurement set across multiple SoC levels while the upstream term is an unspecified generic remaining-power value, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: Schema.org’s endTime is a general property for any event, while our term specifies the end time of temperature exposure.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are dateTime end-time properties and ours is a domain-specific specialization (end of a temperature-exposure interval) of schema.org's generic endTime, so ours is narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate property endDate is a general end date/time for any item, whereas our term specifically denotes the end time of temperature exposure.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are dateTime end-point properties and ours is a domain-specific specialization of schema:endDate (end of a temperature-exposure interval vs. the generic end of any item), making ours strictly narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate property describes a general event location, while our term is restricted to NegativeEvent instances and specifically points to a gs1:Place.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing location; ours is a domain-restricted specialization (NegativeEvent → gs1:Place) of schema.org's general-purpose location property, so ours is narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate property refers to general safety information links, whereas our term specifically denotes end‑of‑life handling guidance for a product.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are string/link properties about product safety, but ours is restricted to end-of-life handling guidance whereas gs1:safetyInfo covers safety information generally, making ours a narrower specialization.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate property refers to general dismantling information, whereas our term specifies an estimated time value for safe battery dismantling.
decisions.qaRationale: Our term is a specific estimated-time quantity, which is one narrower facet of the upstream's undefined but apparently general 'dismantling information', so ours is subsumed by it.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate property refers to the initial internal resistance of a battery, which is a specific case of the general internal resistance described by our term.
decisions.qaRationale: Our general internal-resistance property subsumes the candidate's initial-state (beginning-of-life) internal resistance, so ours is broader.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate property refers to general dismantling information, whereas our term specifies safety instructions for battery dismantling.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties about battery/product dismantling, but ours is restricted to safety-specific instructions whereas the upstream candidate covers dismantling information generally, making ours narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate property denotes general safety measures, while our term specifically addresses safety instructions for battery dismantling.
decisions.qaRationale: Our term is a dismantling-specific safety-instruction URL that plausibly falls under the upstream's general safetyMeasures, but the upstream lacks any definition so the subsumption is uncertain rather than firm.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property indicates a boolean flag for each material, whereas the candidate likely refers to the broader list of critical raw materials.
decisions.qaRationale: Ours is a per-material boolean predicate (range xsd:boolean, domain BatteryMaterial) asserting membership on the EU CRM list, whereas the plural-labeled candidate is structurally a list-valued property enumerating critical raw materials of a product, so they model different concepts rather than a broader/narrower version of the same one.

decisions.bulkRationale: Battery Status is a specific enumeration of battery lifecycle states, whereas StatusEnumeration is a generic class for any status types.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are classes and a battery lifecycle status is plausibly a specialization of schema.org's generic status-enumeration parent, so ours is narrower, though the overlap is thin enough to be borderline.

decisions.bulkRationale: The property specifies a particular recycled content fraction (nickel pre‑consumer share), which is a more specific instance of the generic recycledContent property.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are decimal recycled-content fraction properties, but ours is restricted to one material (nickel) and one source category (pre-consumer), making it a strict specialization of the generic recycledContent property.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties express a fractional value of recycled material, but our term specifically refers to the nickel pre‑consumer share within RecycledContent, whereas the candidate is a generic recycled mass fraction.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are decimal fractional properties expressing recycled material mass share, but ours is a strict specialization restricted to nickel and the pre-consumer sub-stream, whereas the upstream is the generic recycled mass fraction.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties share the same name, domain, and range, indicating they represent the identical concept of a MIME type.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are string-valued properties expressing the identical MIME-type concept; the only difference is eubat's domain restriction, which does not change the property's meaning.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties refer to the proportion of material that comes from renewable sources, but the upstream term lacks a definition and may be broader.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties about the renewable-sourced proportion of a material, but the upstream term has no definition to confirm it is a decimal share rather than a broader descriptor, so they overlap without being provably interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both terms refer to the set of hazardous substances associated with a product, but the upstream definition is unspecified.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing hazardous substances associated with a product, but the upstream term lacks any definition or battery-specific scoping, so identity cannot be confirmed and the weaker overlapping grade is warranted.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate refers to round‑trip efficiency at a fixed number of cycles, whereas our property specifies the efficiency at 50 % of rated cycle life; the scopes differ.
decisions.qaRationale: Our property measures round-trip efficiency at 50% of rated cycle life (a regulation-defined fraction of total lifespan), whereas the candidate measures it after a fixed 50 cycles, so the reference points and resulting values are materially different concepts.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both terms refer to round‑trip efficiency after a portion of the battery’s cycle life, but the candidate lacks the specific 50% life qualifier.
decisions.qaRationale: Both denote round-trip efficiency at a degraded life stage, but ours fixes the specific 50%-of-rated-cycle-life point while the undefined 'remaining' candidate is a generic end-of-life efficiency, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property specifies the recycled nickel share, which is a more specific measure than the generic ‘recycledContent’ property.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties measuring recycled material content, but ours is restricted to the nickel fraction specifically, making it a narrower specialization of the generic recycledContent property.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties refer to a maximum allowed power value, but the candidate lacks a definition and may be broader or more general.
decisions.qaRationale: Both denote a maximum permitted charging/power value for a battery and plausibly align, but the candidate has no definition to confirm it specifically means charging power (not discharge or rated output) and lacks a declared range, so EXACT cannot be justified.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate property lacks a definition and likely refers to a symbol rather than a URL with information about separate collection requirements.
decisions.qaRationale: Our term is a URL pointing to separate-collection requirement information, whereas the candidate denotes a separate-collection symbol/marking, so they reference distinct concepts.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties refer to the energy still available in a product, but the candidate lacks a definition and may be broader or more specific.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are product-energy properties about energy remaining in the item, but the candidate has no definition and 'usable energy' may denote currently available charge rather than our 'current maximum energy' (degraded capacity), so they overlap without being provably interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties refer to the amount of usable energy or capacity left in a product, but the candidate lacks a specific definition and may be broader.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are battery state-of-life properties for what remains in the cell, but ours measures energy (kWh) while the candidate names capacity (typically Ah), which are distinct physical quantities, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties refer to information needed for safe disassembly of a battery, though the candidate lacks a definition.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are battery-dismantling properties, but ours is specifically a document reference (rdfs:Resource) for safe disassembly while the undefined 'dismantlingInformation' likely covers dismantling info more broadly, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate property is a general URL reference, while our term specifies a URL for a due‑diligence report.
decisions.qaRationale: Our property is a specialized case of the generic gs1 referenced-file/URL property, pointing specifically to a supply-chain due-diligence report rather than any resource.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate property represents generic recycled content, while our term specifies the post‑consumer cobalt share, a more specific subset.
decisions.qaRationale: Our term is a specific subset (post-consumer cobalt fraction) of the candidate's generic recycled-content property, both being decimal-valued properties, so ours is narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties refer to the name of a standard (e.g., ISO), but one is for verification and the other for certification.
decisions.qaRationale: Both name a conformity-assessment standard as a free-text string, but verification and certification are distinct conformity activities (ISO/IEC 17000), so they overlap without being interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: The upstream property represents generic recycled content, while our property specifies the nickel share of post‑consumer recycled material.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are recycled-content fraction properties, but ours restricts to a single material (nickel) and a single source tier (post-consumer), making it a strict specialization of the generic upstream property.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property specifies the post‑consumer recycled share of nickel, which is a more specific instance of the generic recycled mass fraction concept.
decisions.qaRationale: Our property restricts the generic recycled mass fraction to nickel material and post-consumer source only, making it a strict specialization of the upstream term.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate property represents a general ISO 8601 duration, while our term specifies a numeric minute count for temperature exposure.
decisions.qaRationale: Our term is a specific kind of duration (temperature exposure, counted in integer minutes) that is conceptually narrower than schema:duration's generic ISO 8601 time-length property, so a narrowMatch holds despite differing representation.

decisions.bulkRationale: eubat:batteryMass is a specific sub-property of gs1:netWeight, describing only battery mass rather than general net weight.
decisions.qaRationale: Battery mass is the net weight of a specific product type (a battery), so it is a narrower instance of the same net-weight concept measured on the same domain and range.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both terms refer to information that guides battery dismantling, though the candidate lacks a defined range and may be broader or more generic.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties about battery dismantling guidance, but 'documents' (supporting artifacts) and 'instructions' (procedural guidance) are overlapping rather than interchangeable concepts, and the undefined upstream term blocks any stronger claim.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both describe capacity in ampere‑hours, but the candidate property covers all fuel storage types (fuel tank or battery), whereas our term is specific to a battery’s rated capacity.
decisions.qaRationale: Both can be expressed in ampere-hours, but schema:fuelCapacity is a broad vehicle-storage property (fuel tank or EV battery total) while eubat:ratedCapacity is the specific rated capacity of a battery, making ours narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our class specifies a battery‑specific carbon footprint performance classification (A–E), which is a more specific instance of the generic EmissionsPerformance concept.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are classes and our battery-specific carbon footprint A–E classification is a specialized kind of the generic emissions-performance concept, so ours is narrower, though the upstream term's missing definition leaves some residual uncertainty.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both terms share the same label, IRI, and domain/range context.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing the production-phase carbon footprint value with matching label and semantics, with OUR eubat term anchoring upward to its oec core counterpart.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property focuses specifically on end‑of‑life handling for batteries, while the candidate covers general sustainability and recycling information.
decisions.qaRationale: Our battery end-of-life handling/dismantling/recycling/disposal property is a more specific child of GS1's general sustainability-and-recycling-information link, so ours is narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate property covers general consumer handling and storage information, while our property is specifically about end‑of‑life handling, making ours a narrower concept.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties about safe handling instructions, but ours covers end-of-life dismantling, recycling, and disposal whereas the GS1 candidate covers consumer in-use handling and storage, so the two address different lifecycle phases and overlap only partially rather than ours being a clean subset.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both terms share the same label and are intended to represent the same property within the carbon footprint context.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties representing the distribution-stage carbon footprint value; the eubat term is the regulation-layer alias of the identical oec core property, making them interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate property covers general sustainability and recycling information, while our property specifically points to waste prevention details.
decisions.qaRationale: Our waste-prevention link is one specific facet of the candidate's broader sustainability-and-recycling information, so ours is narrower than the GS1 property.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property is a specific URL pointing to a label symbol image, while the candidate represents any generic URL of an item.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties yielding a URL, but ours is a constrained subtype pointing specifically to a label symbol image whereas schema:url is the generic URL of any item, making ours strictly narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties refer to a threshold indicating battery exhaustion, though the upstream term uses “capacity” while ours specifies a percentage.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing a battery-exhaustion threshold, but ours is specifically an SOCE percentage while the undefined upstream term is framed around capacity, so they overlap without being provably interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property covers any facility identifier, whereas the candidate is specifically a GS1 Global Location Number.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties identifying a facility/location, but ours accepts any identifier scheme (xsd:string, GLN merely recommended) whereas gs1:globalLocationNumber denotes specifically the GS1 GLN key, so ours subsumes the candidate.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties provide a URL to third‑party verification or assurance information.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties named thirdPartyAssurancesUrl carrying a URL to third-party verification/assurance; the upstream oec: term is the core counterpart our eubat: term anchors to, and despite the missing upstream definition the identical name and matching property semantics make them interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties refer to a document containing dismantling instructions, but the candidate lacks a definition and may be broader or less specific to EU Battery Regulation.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are document-reference properties for dismantling instructions and overlap heavily, but ours is battery-specific and additionally scopes 'removal' while the undefined upstream term is generic dismantling-only, so they overlap without being a clean subset or equivalent.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties represent the certification or accreditation of a data provider, and share the same label and domain/range context.
decisions.qaRationale: Same property name in this project's own core layer, identical label, and the eubat term is a regulation-level alias of the oec core term for data provider certification.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property specifies a URL for a declaration of conformity, which is a specific type of supporting evidence; the candidate property generically links any supporting evidence to a requirement.
decisions.qaRationale: A declaration-of-conformity URL is one specific kind of supporting evidence for a requirement, so our property is narrower than the generic hasSupportingEvidence link, though the range differs (anyURI vs evidence resource).

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property specifies a URL for an EU Declaration of Conformity, while the candidate is a generic URL property.
decisions.qaRationale: Our property is a specialized URL (specifically the EU Declaration of Conformity document link) and is thus a narrower form of schema.org's generic url property, but both are properties carrying a URL value so the kinship is real.

decisions.bulkRationale: Hazardous Substance is a specific type of chemical substance, thus it is narrower than the general ChemicalSubstance class.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are classes and our hazardous substance is a CLP-classified subset of the general chemical substance concept, hence narrower, though our term may also span mixtures that schema:ChemicalSubstance's constant-composition definition excludes.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties represent the Chemical Abstracts Service registry number for a chemical substance.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties capturing the Chemical Abstracts Service registry number string, an unambiguous identifier concept, with ours simply being the battery-scoped alias of the core term.

decisions.bulkRationale: The upstream property describes the chemical composition of any substance, whereas ours is limited to the cathode active material within a battery chemistry.
decisions.qaRationale: Our property restricts schema:chemicalComposition to the cathode active material of a battery, so it is a narrower specialization of the general chemical-composition property.

decisions.bulkRationale: The upstream property denotes general certification information, while ours specifies third‑party verification of a carbon footprint declaration.
decisions.qaRationale: Our property attaches third-party verification specifically to a carbon footprint declaration, a narrower specialization of schema.org's general product/organization certification property.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property specifies the impact of a temperature excursion on battery health, which is a specific instance of a general hazard impact.
decisions.qaRationale: A temperature excursion is a type of hazard, so our battery-specific estimated impact is a narrower specialization of the general hazardImpact property, though the upstream term's missing definition limits certainty.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties refer to the rate at which a battery self‑discharges, though the upstream term lacks a definition and unit specification.
decisions.qaRationale: Both concern battery self-discharge, but the undefined upstream label 'selfDischargeCurrent' may denote self-discharge electrical current (amperes) rather than our percent-per-month rate, so overlap is plausible but identity is unconfirmed.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate property represents general recycled content, while the OUR term specifies a quantitative share of recycled lithium.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing recycled-content share, but ours is restricted specifically to lithium whereas the upstream covers recycled content generally, making ours narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both describe a recycled mass fraction, but ours is specific to lithium content.
decisions.qaRationale: Ours measures recycled mass fraction restricted to lithium content, a proper subconcept of UNTP's general recycledMassFraction, so ours is narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate property is a generic URL reference, whereas our property specifically denotes an incident report URL.
decisions.qaRationale: Our property is a specialized URL reference (to an incident report) that is subsumed by GS1's generic referenced-file/resource URL, so ours is narrower; the link is genuine but weak since the target carries no incident semantics.

decisions.bulkRationale: Battery Material is a specific type of general Material used in battery construction, making it narrower than the generic Untp Material.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are material-composition classes, but our BatteryMaterial specializes the generic UNTP Material with battery-specific construction use, CAS/EC identifiers, and sourcing, making ours strictly narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: Battery Material is a specific type of Substance used in batteries, making it a narrower subclass.
decisions.qaRationale: BatteryMaterial is matter of defined composition (a Substance) specialized to battery construction with composition/CAS-EC/sourcing attributes, so ours is a genuine narrower subclass of the broader schema:Substance.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties refer to the number of battery cycles a product is expected to endure, but the candidate lacks a definition and may be broader.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing a product's expected battery cycle count, but the candidate has no definition to confirm it means full charge-discharge cycles, so interchangeability cannot be asserted.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties refer to the efficiency of a round‑trip energy process, though the candidate lacks a definition and may imply remaining efficiency.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are battery round-trip efficiency properties on the same domain, but ours measures current absolute efficiency while the candidate's 'Remaining' suffix signals a distinct present-vs-rated retention metric, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties describe chemical composition, but ours is specific to battery electrolyte whereas the candidate covers any substance.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing chemical composition, but ours is constrained to a battery's electrolyte while schema:chemicalComposition applies to any substance, making ours a narrower specialization.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both refer to the European Community EC number, but the candidate term is framed for a component rather than a substance.
decisions.qaRationale: Both encode the EINECS/ELINCS EC number identifier, but ours attaches it to a SubstanceOfConcern while the undefined upstream term frames it for a component, so the property meaning overlaps without being interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property specifies distribution‑stage emissions, a subset of the generic carbon footprint concept.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties yielding a carbon-footprint quantity, and distribution-stage emissions are a stage-scoped specialization of the generic product carbon footprint, so ours is narrower—though the relation is partly partitive (one stage of a multi-stage declaration) which weakens confidence.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our class represents a full type‑III EPD, while the candidate denotes an individual block within such a declaration.
decisions.qaRationale: Both classes concern the EPD concept, but 'EPDBlock' (no definition) most plausibly names the passport's EPD container/section rather than a sub-block of a declaration, so the first-pass BROAD reading is unsupported and an overlapping CLOSE match is the defensible grade.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property specifies a URL for a carbon footprint study, which is a more specific use of the generic website URL defined upstream.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties carrying a URL to a referenced document, and ours is a semantically constrained subtype (specifically the carbon footprint study) of the generic gs1:referencedFileURL, so ours is narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property specifies production‑stage emissions with a QuantitativeValue range, whereas the candidate is a generic carbon footprint property.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties carrying a carbon-footprint value, but ours is restricted to one lifecycle stage (production) whereas the untp term is a generic, whole-product carbon footprint, making ours a narrower specialization.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property specifies the URI of a compostability certification standard, which is a more specific instance of the generic certification standard defined upstream.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties naming the standard a claim is certified against, but ours is restricted to compostability certifications while gs1:certificationStandard covers any certification standard, making ours a narrower instance of the same concept.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property provides specific safe‑use instructions for a substance of concern, which is a narrower instance of the broader consumer safety information defined upstream.
decisions.qaRationale: Safe-use instructions for a substance of concern are a specific subtype of the broader consumer safety information, so our property is narrower than the upstream one.

decisions.bulkRationale: TraceabilityPerformance is a specific type of performance metric, whereas PerformanceMetric is a general class.
decisions.qaRationale: Our class is one specific performance-metric category (proportion of materials verifiably traced), making it a subtype of UNTP's generic PerformanceMetric class, so ours is narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: The SCIP ID is a specific type of identifier, whereas schema.org’s identifier property covers all kinds of identifiers.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties and a SCIP database identifier is a specific instance of schema.org's generic identifier concept, so ours is genuinely narrower than the catch-all target.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties provide repair or maintenance instructions, but the GS1 term focuses on a link to service info while ours is broader repairability information.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties conveying repair/maintenance information, but GS1 serviceInfo is a simple hyperlink to service instructions while ours references a structured RepairabilityInfo object, so they overlap without being interchangeable or cleanly nested.

decisions.bulkRationale: The upstream property represents recycled content in general, while our term specifies the pre‑consumer (post‑industrial) fraction.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are recycled-content properties, but ours measures only the pre-consumer (post-industrial) fraction, a strict subset of the upstream's overall recycled content, so our term is narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate property is a general identifier for any Thing, while our term specifies an identifier specifically for material components.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are identifier properties and ours is a domain-restricted specialization of schema:identifier's generic any-Thing identifier, so ours is the narrower concept.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties link to a prior version of the same object, though our term is specific to passports while the upstream term refers generally to assets.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties asserting a link to the immediately prior version of the same entity, but adms:prev is domain-scoped to ADMS Assets (semantic assets) rather than passports, so the predecessor-version semantics overlap without being fully interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property refers specifically to the jurisdiction of an EPR registration, a narrower concept than the general legal jurisdiction described by the candidate.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing a jurisdiction relation, and our term is a domain-constrained specialization (the country/member-state of an EPR registration) of schema.org's general legal-jurisdiction property, justifying a broadMatch/NARROW grade.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties quantify the recyclable portion of a product, but the candidate term lacks the explicit decimal fraction definition and domain specification.
decisions.qaRationale: Both quantify a recycling-related fraction of a product, but recyclabilityRate measures the portion that *can be* recycled (end-of-life recyclability) whereas untp:recycledContent measures the portion *already made from* recycled material (input content), so they are related but not the same concept.

decisions.bulkRationale: The GS1 ‘Instructions’ property is a general link to any instructions, whereas our property specifically refers to repair instructions and manuals.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are link-type properties; GS1 'instructions' is a generic link to any instructions (assembly, usage, etc.) of which repair instructions is one specific subtype, so our term is properly narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: The GS1 ‘Service information’ link covers all service or maintenance instructions, while our property specifically targets repair instructions and manuals.
decisions.qaRationale: GS1 serviceInfo links broadly to service or maintenance instructions, and repair instructions are a narrower subset of that documentation scope, so ours is the more specific concept.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties represent the systematic IUPAC chemical name of a substance.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties carrying the systematic IUPAC chemical name of a substance with identical meaning and string range, differing only in domain scope which does not affect property semantics.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate property refers to an IUPAC name of a component within a product, whereas our term specifies the IUPAC name of an individual hazardous substance.
decisions.qaRationale: Both properties carry the systematic IUPAC name of a chemical entity and their string values are interchangeable, but ours is scoped to a hazardous substance while the candidate targets a generic product component, so they overlap without being identical.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties represent the same concept of emissions performance data, with identical labels and aligned definitions.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties carrying an emissions-performance data container and OUR term explicitly mirrors UNTP's emissionsPerformance 1:1 (same role, same range class), making them interchangeable despite the upstream lacking an inline definition.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our CAS Number property applies to any hazardous substance, whereas the upstream term refers specifically to a component’s CAS number.
decisions.qaRationale: Both record the same CAS registry identifier so they overlap strongly, but they attach to different (and not cleanly nested) bearers — our hazardous-substance vs. their component — and the upstream term lacks a definition, so a clean BROAD subsumption can't be asserted.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property captures only raw‑material acquisition emissions, whereas the upstream term represents the overall carbon footprint.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are carbon-footprint properties, but ours scopes a single lifecycle stage (raw-material acquisition) which is a more granular slice of the upstream term's overall product carbon footprint.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate property provides general certification information, while OUR property specifically refers to the certification of a data provider.
decisions.qaRationale: Both express certification, but ours is scoped specifically to the data provider (a kind of organization) whereas schema:hasCertification covers certification of any product, organization, service, place, or person, making ours a strict specialization.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate property covers general certification for products, organizations or locations, whereas OUR property is specifically about the certification of a data provider.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are certification properties and a data provider is an organisation, so OUR term is a narrower, role-specific specialization of GS1's general certification of a product/organisation/location.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property refers specifically to an impact indicator result within an environmental product declaration, whereas the upstream term denotes a generic indicator.
decisions.qaRationale: A generic upstream 'indicator' property plausibly subsumes our EPD-specific impactIndicator, making ours the narrower term, but the upstream definition is absent so the conceptual match cannot be confirmed beyond name similarity.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our class specifies substances of concern per SCIP/ESPR, whereas the upstream term is a generic substance.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are classes; a substance of concern is a regulatory subset of the generic upstream Substance, so ours is narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: Dismantling instructions are a specific type of end‑of‑life instruction, making our property narrower than the upstream endOfLifeInstructions.
decisions.qaRationale: Dismantling is one specific end-of-life operation, so our property is a genuine narrower (subset) case of the upstream endOfLifeInstructions, both being properties.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties record the date an item was issued, though one refers to a digital product passport and the other to a ticket.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are date-issued properties with range date, but schema:dateIssued is scoped to tickets/credentials while ours specifically records DPP issuance, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property specifies a URL for authorized repair service providers, which is a more specific use of the generic contact page concept.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties pointing to a web page for contact/service access, and ours is a specialized subset (authorized repair providers) of the generic upstream contact page, so it is narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property refers specifically to certifications held by a facility, whereas the candidate property is a general certification attribute for any product, organization or location.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties asserting held certifications, but ours is restricted to facility (location/organisation) subjects whereas gs1:certification spans products, organisations, and locations, making ours a proper subset.

decisions.bulkRationale: hasCertification is a general property for any entity, while Facility Certifications specifically applies to facilities.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties asserting certifications on an entity, but ours is scoped specifically to facilities whereas schema:hasCertification ranges over products, organizations, services, places, and persons, making ours a narrower specialization.

decisions.bulkRationale: Facility Certifications is a specific property for facility-held certificates, whereas Certification Information is a general link to any certification data.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties linking to certification data, but oec:facilityCertifications is a facility-scoped specialization of gs1:certificationInfo's general certification link, making ours narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: The economic operator responsible for a product includes manufacturers, distributors, importers, etc., whereas ‘Has Manufacturer’ refers only to the producing organization.
decisions.qaRationale: Our 'economic operator responsible for the product' is a regulatory super-category that subsumes manufacturer along with importer, distributor and authorised representative, so ours is strictly broader than gs1:manufacturer (the producing organization only).

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties denote a version identifier for an object, but the schema.org property is generic to CreativeWork while ours is specific to a Digital Product Passport.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are version-identifier properties of a resource, but schema.org scopes its property to CreativeWork while ours scopes to a Digital Product Passport, and a DPP is not unambiguously a CreativeWork, so they overlap without identity or a safe subsumption claim.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties provide a URL for returning or taking back a product, but the upstream term focuses on merchant returns while ours specifies consumer-facing take‑back program pages.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties giving a URL for product return/take-back, but schema.org's merchantReturnLink targets commercial merchant returns while ours denotes a consumer-facing end-of-life take-back program, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate property describes textile material composition with percentages, a specific instance of the more general material composition concept represented by our property.
decisions.qaRationale: Our generic material-composition property subsumes the textile-specific GS1 property, which is scoped to textile material content with percentages, so ours is broader.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate class covers all energy‑consumption information for a product, while our class focuses specifically on the declared efficiency levels and certifications.
decisions.qaRationale: Our class is the EU A-G/EPREL efficiency-declaration subset of schema.org's broader EnergyConsumptionDetails, which spans all regulation-based energy-consumption information for an energy-using product.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both classes describe product performance metrics, but the OUR term specifically includes lifespan and durability per ESPR Article 7, whereas the candidate is a more generic performance metric.
decisions.qaRationale: Ours is an ESPR-scoped information container aggregating lifespan, usage cycles, and metrics, while the upstream is a single atomic performance metric, so they overlap conceptually but are not interchangeable nor cleanly broader/narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: Performance Information encompasses durability plus additional performance metrics, whereas Durability refers only to the product’s endurance.
decisions.qaRationale: Our PerformanceInfo covers durability plus broader performance metrics (lifespan, usage cycles), so durability is a proper subset, making ours broader; both are classes.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property refers specifically to an EPR scheme registration number, whereas the candidate is a general link to any register entry.
decisions.qaRationale: An EPR compliance-scheme producer registration number is a specific instance of the general 'entry in a register that acts as an alternative identifier' that gs1:registryEntry models, so ours is the narrower concept.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property is a specific identifier for a DataElement within its container, whereas the candidate is a generic identifier applicable to any Thing.
decisions.qaRationale: oec:elementId is a specific kind of identifier (a container-scoped element id) and thus a proper subtype of schema:identifier's generic any-Thing identifier.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties are URLs, but reportUrl is specifically for a due diligence report, whereas referencedFileURL is a generic website reference.
decisions.qaRationale: oec:reportUrl is a specialized slot for a due diligence report's URL, a proper subset of gs1:referencedFileURL's generic any-resource file reference, so ours is strictly narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property refers specifically to waste prevention information, while the candidate covers broader sustainability and recycling content.
decisions.qaRationale: Our waste-prevention URL property is a specific subset of the candidate's broader sustainability-and-recycling link, so ours is narrower than the GS1 term.

decisions.bulkRationale: The property covers any performance or efficiency class, whereas the candidate is specifically for energy efficiency.
decisions.qaRationale: Our property covers any performance or efficiency class while the candidate is restricted to energy efficiency, so ours subsumes it (both are properties).

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property specifies a Decentralized Identifier (DID), whereas the candidate is a general identifier for any Thing.
decisions.qaRationale: A DID is one specific kind of identifier, so oec:did is a narrower property under schema:identifier's general identifier concept.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our class specifies the EU Energy Labelling Regulation A‑G classes, whereas the candidate covers a wider range of international energy efficiency levels.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are classes denoting energy-efficiency classification scales, but ours is restricted to the EU 2017/1369 A-G classes while schema:EnergyEfficiencyEnumeration spans multiple international energy-efficiency standards, making ours a proper subset.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property specifies a URL for a directory of physical collection points, which is a more specific use case than the generic referencedFileURL that can point to any internet resource.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are property-type URLs and ours points to a specific kind of internet resource (a collection-point directory), making it a genuine narrowing of GS1's generic referencedFileURL/Website rather than a separate concept.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property is a numeric indicator value for a lifecycle stage, which is a more specific use of the generic measurement value defined in GS1.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are unit-qualified numeric-value properties, and our stageValue is a domain-specific specialization of GS1's generic measurement value, making ours genuinely narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: The candidate property denotes recycled content in general, while the OUR term specifies the post‑consumer fraction only.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing recycled-content fraction, but ours restricts to the post-consumer subset whereas the untp term covers recycled content generally, making ours narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: EmissionsPerformance is a specific type of Performance, focusing on greenhouse gas data.
decisions.qaRationale: Our emissions-specific container is one specialization of UNTP's generic abstract Performance class (which also subsumes circularity, traceability, etc.), so ours is properly narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: EmissionsPerformance focuses specifically on greenhouse gas data, whereas EnvironmentalFootprint is a broader concept that can include multiple environmental impact categories.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are classes, and our GHG-emissions-specific container is a proper subset of the broader multi-category EnvironmentalFootprint concept, so ours is narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: passportIdentifier is a specific URI for a DPP instance, while schema.org identifier is a general property for any kind of identifier.
decisions.qaRationale: oec:passportIdentifier is a specialized identifier property for a single DPP instance, semantically subsumed by schema.org's generic identifier property for any Thing, so ours is the narrower property.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties use IETF BCP 47 language tags (ISO 639 + region), but schema.org inLanguage refers to content or action, whereas our property is a language tag of an arbitrary value.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties asserting the language of something via standard language codes, but oec:language is a generic value-level language tag whereas schema:inLanguage scopes specifically to the language of content or an action, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: The GS1 sustainabilityInfo property links to general sustainability and recycling information, whereas our end‑of‑life instructions specifically provide a URL for end‑of‑life handling.
decisions.qaRationale: Our end-of-life handling instructions URL is a specific subset of GS1's broad sustainability-and-recycling information link, so ours is narrower than the upstream property.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our DocumentReference encompasses any external document (reports, manuals, guides) in multiple languages and formats, whereas ReferencedFileDetails is a narrower class that specifically provides URL and file‑specific information.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are classes pointing to an external file/document with URL and metadata, but neither is clearly broader since GS1's ReferencedFileDetails covers any electronic file (not only documents) while ours is document-scoped yet adds language handling, so they overlap without one subsuming the other.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties record a version identifier, but the DPP Schema Version is specific to DigitalProductPassport instances, whereas schema.org’s version applies generally to any CreativeWork.
decisions.qaRationale: oec:dppSchemaVersion records a version identifier like schema.org/version but is constrained to the schema/standard a DigitalProductPassport conforms to, a narrower instance of the general CreativeWork version property.

decisions.bulkRationale: The upstream term Substance is a generic class of any substance, whereas our HazardousSubstance refers specifically to substances declared under EU CLP Regulation.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are classes and our hazardous-substance concept (CLP-declared) is a proper subset of the upstream generic Substance class, so ours is narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: The upstream term describes any component location in a product, while our Substance Location specifically denotes the location where a particular substance is present.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are string properties locating a constituent within a product, but ours locates a substance of concern while theirs locates a component, which are different entity types, so NARROW is unjustified and only an approximate overlap holds.

decisions.bulkRationale: The upstream role property applies to any agent in a participation, whereas our operatorRole specifically denotes the role of an economic operator.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing the function/role an actor plays; ours restricts the generic agent-in-participation role to specifically the economic operator's role, making it a genuine narrowing of the upstream term.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties refer to substances considered regulatory concerns present in a product, though our definition specifies database requirements and explicit presence.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties flagging product-contained substances of regulatory concern, but GS1's 'ingredient' (intentionally-added component, claim-based) and our 'substance' (any SVHC present per SCIP/ESPR) frame overlapping-not-identical scopes, so CLOSE rather than EXACT.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties record the date of a compliance-related audit or verification, but one specifies a general compliance check while the other explicitly references a certification audit.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are date properties marking a compliance/audit verification event, overlapping in meaning but not interchangeable since auditDate is scoped to a certification's last audit while complianceDate generically records compliance verification.

decisions.bulkRationale: The OUR property covers multiple industry classification systems (ISIC, NACE, NAICS), whereas the candidate property refers only to NAICS.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties, and our term generalizes across multiple industry classification schemes (ISIC, NACE, NAICS) while schema:naics is specifically the NAICS code, making ours strictly broader.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property covers multiple industry classification schemes (ISIC, NACE, NAICS), whereas the candidate is specific to ISIC v4.
decisions.qaRationale: Our property generically captures any industry-activity classification code (ISIC, NACE, NAICS), whereas schema:isicV4 is restricted to the single ISIC Revision 4 scheme, so ours properly subsumes the candidate.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties record a date/time when data was updated or published, but the candidate refers specifically to structured‑data generation rather than a generic DPP data update.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are record-level metadata timestamps about the data itself rather than the product, but ours marks the last update of DPP data generically while sdDatePublished specifically marks when the current structured-data serialization was generated/published, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both classes describe the granularity of a product identifier (model, batch, or item), though our definition is specific to EN 18223 and EPCIS4DPP.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are classes enumerating the same model/batch/item granularity tristate, but ours is framed as passport-level granularity anchored to EN 18223 and EPCIS4DPP Digital Link AIs while UNTP's is product-identifier granularity, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property specifies a URL for a document, while the GS1 property refers to any internet resource; thus our term is a narrower specialization.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties supplying a URL; ours is constrained to a document reference while GS1 referencedFileURL covers any internet resource (documents, people, etc.), so our term is a narrower specialization.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property points to a specific URL for separate collection requirements, which is a subset of the broader sustainability and recycling information link.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties linking to external information; separate-collection requirements are one specific subset of GS1's broader sustainability-and-recycling link, so ours is narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both classes represent a manufacturing or processing facility, but our definition includes ESPR‑specific attributes and GS1 Digital Link usage.
decisions.qaRationale: Our ESPR-specific FacilityInformation, scoped to manufacturing/processing facilities with regulatory certification data, is a specialized subtype of UNTP's generic Facility class, so ours is narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both classes describe a manufacturing or processing facility, but our class specifies ESPR‑based information and GS1 identifiers.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are classes denoting a manufacturing/processing facility, overlapping in concept, but ours is an ESPR-scoped information record keyed to GS1 identifiers rather than UNTP's generic Facility entity, so they are related but not interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both classes refer to a manufacturing or processing facility, but our class specifies ESPR‑based details and GS1 identifiers.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are facility classes, but ours is an ESPR-scoped information container inheriting from gs1:Place while the upstream's lack of a definition leaves exact scope unverifiable, so overlapping-not-identical is the safest grade.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our class represents a specific type of place (manufacturing or processing facility) with additional attributes, making it narrower than the general schema.org Place.
decisions.qaRationale: Our class models a manufacturing/processing facility as a gs1:Place subtype, which is a specific kind of the general schema:Place, so ours is narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: Facility Information is a specific type of Place, thus it is narrower than the generic Place class.
decisions.qaRationale: A manufacturing/processing facility is a specialized subtype of the generic gs1:Place (and explicitly inherits gln/name/address from it), so our class is narrower than the upstream class.

decisions.bulkRationale: Operator Information specifies economic operator roles (manufacturer, importer, distributor) identified by GLN, which is a more specific subset of the broader GS1 Organization concept.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are classes; our term scopes the broad gs1:Organization down to the regulatory subset of economic operators (manufacturer/importer/distributor) identified by GLN, making ours strictly narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our class specifies economic operator roles (manufacturer, importer, distributor) whereas the upstream Party is a generic entity.
decisions.qaRationale: OperatorInformation is a role-constrained subtype of party (economic operators only) inheriting from gs1:Organization, so it is narrower than the generic untp:Party class.

decisions.bulkRationale: OperatorInformation is a specific type of Actor that represents economic operators in EU regulations, making it narrower than the generic Actor class.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are classes for supply-chain parties and our economic-operator concept (manufacturer/importer/distributor, identified by GLN as a gs1:Organization) is a specific subtype of the generic, undefined upstream Actor.

decisions.bulkRationale: Operator Information is a specialized class of economic operators (manufacturers, importers, distributors), which are specific types of Organization.
decisions.qaRationale: OperatorInformation inherits from gs1:Organization and represents economic operators (manufacturer, importer, distributor), a regulatory subset of the very general schema:Organization, so ours is narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: OperatorInformation includes all economic operator roles, while ManufacturerRecord is a specific subset for manufacturers.
decisions.qaRationale: Our OperatorInformation class covers all economic operator roles (manufacturer, importer, distributor), so it subsumes a manufacturer-specific record, though the upstream term's missing definition limits certainty.

decisions.bulkRationale: OperatorInformation represents specific economic operator roles, whereas LegalEntity is a general legal entity concept.
decisions.qaRationale: An economic operator is a legal entity acting in specific regulated roles (manufacturer, importer, distributor), making OperatorInformation a class-to-class hyponym of the general LegalEntity concept.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our term specifies a classification system for customs commodity codes, whereas the upstream Type Code is a generic code list.
decisions.qaRationale: A customs-commodity-code classification scheme is a specific kind of the generic GS1 code-list type, so ours is a narrower specialization, though the upstream class is an abstract placeholder making the anchor weak.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties refer to a URL that points to a verifiable digital identity credential, but the upstream term is more specific to a Digital Identity Anchor.
decisions.qaRationale: Our generic 'URL to an identity verifiable credential' overlaps the UNTP DigitalIdentityAnchor concept but is broader and less specific, so it is not an exact interchangeable match.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties refer to an energy‑efficiency attribute, but the candidate is a specific classification property while our term is a generic energy‑efficiency indicator.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are energy-efficiency properties that overlap, but the candidate denotes a specific efficiency classification (e.g. A–G label) while our undefined term reads as a generic efficiency attribute, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties refer to the energy efficiency category of a product, but our term is generic while the candidate specifies it as part of consumption details.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing a product's energy efficiency class/rating, but ours points directly to an efficiency value while schema:hasEnergyConsumptionDetails links to a broader consumption-details object, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: EPREL Registration Number is a specific type of regulatory reference number, whereas regulatoryReferenceNumber covers any record in any regulatory system.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties carrying a registry reference number, and the EPREL registration number is a specific instance of the generic regulatoryReferenceNumber, so ours is narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: The upstream property has no definition and its label (avsSystem) does not provide enough evidence to confirm it matches the AVCP system concept.
decisions.qaRationale: The upstream property has no definition and its label 'avsSystem' (AVS) does not reliably correspond to the AVCP-system concept, so a match cannot be confirmed.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties denote the date an item is issued, though the specific domain (Declaration vs. ticket) differs.
decisions.qaRationale: Both denote the issuance date of a formal credential/document, but schema.org dateIssued is scoped to tickets/certifications while ours is specific to a Declaration of Performance, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: Detergent Product is a specific type of product, making it a narrower subclass of the general GS1 Product.
decisions.qaRationale: DetergentProduct is a regulation-specific subclass of the general GS1 Product (any priceable/orderable item), so ours is strictly narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: Fragrance Allergen is a specific type of chemical substance that must be disclosed, whereas ChemicalSubstance is the general class of all chemical substances.
decisions.qaRationale: A fragrance allergen identified by name and CAS number is genuinely a kind of chemical substance, so ours is a narrower (more specialized, disclosure-scoped) class of schema.org's general ChemicalSubstance.

decisions.bulkRationale: Dosage Instructions is a specific type of consumer usage instructions, adding water‑hardness guidance.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are free-text product-instruction properties, and dosage instructions (with water-hardness guidance) are a specific subset of the broader consumer usage instructions, so OUR term is narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties represent a numeric dose or dosage value, but our term specifies recommended usage per use while the candidate is a generic dose value.
decisions.qaRationale: Both express a dose amount, but schema:doseValue is a bare numeric value rooted in medication DoseSchedule whereas ours is a unit-bearing recommended per-use detergent quantity, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: eudet:ingredientList specifies a structured list of Ingredient entities, whereas GS1’s ingredientStatement is a single string; thus our property is a narrower, more detailed version.
decisions.qaRationale: Both convey the product's constituent ingredients, but eudet:ingredientList is a structured list of typed Ingredient entities under INCI nomenclature whereas GS1's ingredientStatement is a single free-text string, making ours a narrower, more specialized expression of the same concept.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties represent a numeric rating value, but displayScore is specifically for repairability while ratingValue is generic.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are decimal rating properties, but displayScore is a domain-specific repairability sub-score on a fixed 0-10 scale whereas schema:ratingValue is a generic rating value, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: RoHS Compliance is a specific type of certification, whereas hasCertification covers all certifications.
decisions.qaRationale: RoHS compliance is one specific certification type, so as a property it is narrower than the general hasCertification property covering all certifications.

decisions.bulkRationale: RoHS Compliance is a specific type of certification, whereas Certification Information is a generic link to any certification.
decisions.qaRationale: RoHS Compliance is a specific regulatory compliance/certification topic, which is narrower than a generic link to any certification information, so ours is the narrower term.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both provide explanatory text for a rating/criterion score, but the schema property is intended for short explanations while ours allows detailed descriptions.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties carrying free-text explanation of a score, but schema:ratingExplanation is bound to a Rating (and fact-check context) while ours explains a repair-criterion score, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: WEEE registration is a specific EPR scheme, whereas eprRegistrationNumber refers to producer registration for any EPR program.
decisions.qaRationale: WEEE registration is a specific instance of EPR producer registration, so our term is a narrower specialization of the general eprRegistrationNumber property.

decisions.bulkRationale: euelec:SoftwareSupport covers all OS/firmware support details, while dppk’s softwareUpdatePolicy is a specific policy about updates; the former is a broader concept.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are classes; our SoftwareSupport spans the full OS/firmware support picture (update periods, patches, end-of-support dates) whereas dppk's softwareUpdatePolicy is confined to update policy, a subset of that scope.

decisions.bulkRationale: euelec:modelIdentifier specifies a textual model number, while schema.org’s model accepts either a URL or text; the upstream term is more general.
decisions.qaRationale: schema:model denotes the product's model and may reference a full ProductModel entity or URL as well as a textual model identifier, so our text-only manufacturer model number is a narrower subset of it.

decisions.bulkRationale: euelec:modelIdentifier specifies a manufacturer’s model number, whereas schema.org’s identifier is a generic property for any kind of identifier; the upstream term is more general.
decisions.qaRationale: A manufacturer's model number is a specific kind of the generic schema.org identifier property, so our term is narrower than the upstream term.

decisions.bulkRationale: euelec:componentPassport refers specifically to a component’s own nested DPP, whereas gs1:Digital Product Passport is a general link to any product passport; the upstream term is more general.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are properties whose value is a link to a digital product passport; ours restricts that link to the specific case of a component's nested DPP, making it a narrower specialization of the general gs1:dpp link.

decisions.bulkRationale: The upstream property has no definition and its label does not indicate a WEEE registration country.
decisions.qaRationale: WEEE registration is a specific EPR scheme, so our WEEE registration country is a narrower instance of the upstream EPR jurisdiction (country where an EPR registration applies).

decisions.bulkRationale: The upstream property describes a general price specification, while ours specifies the price of a component used as a spare part.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are price-association properties pointing to a PriceSpecification; ours restricts the general schema:priceSpecification to the specific spare-part price, making it a genuine sub-property (narrower).

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties capture a version identifier for an entity, though schema.org’s applies to CreativeWork while ours is generic software/firmware.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are version-identifier properties, but ours denotes specifically the post-update software/firmware version (a role within an update event) rather than schema.org's generic CreativeWork version, so they overlap without being interchangeable or a clean subtype.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our class represents a specific repairability criterion, while the upstream class is a generic evaluation condition.
decisions.qaRationale: Our class is a domain-specific repairability criterion that is a true specialization of the generic CCCEV evaluation Criterion, so ours is narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our class is a specific repairability criterion, whereas the upstream class represents a generic evaluation condition.
decisions.qaRationale: A repairability index sub-criterion is a specific species of UNTP's generic evaluation Criterion, so ours is narrower, though the upstream's conformity-assessment framing only partially overlaps our scoring-index sense.

decisions.bulkRationale: RepairCriterion is a specific type of numeric score, whereas Rating is a general concept for any numeric evaluation.
decisions.qaRationale: A repair criterion is a domain-specific numeric evaluation score, making it a specialized subtype of schema:Rating's general numeric-evaluation concept.

decisions.bulkRationale: euelec:osVersion specifies the version of an operating system, which is a specific type of software instance; schema.org/softwareVersion refers to any software version.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are version-string properties, and an operating system is a specific kind of software instance, so euelec:osVersion is a narrower specialization of schema.org/softwareVersion.

decisions.bulkRationale: euelec:EnergyEfficiency focuses on EU Energy Labeling Regulation data, whereas schema.org/EnergyEfficiencyEnumeration covers a broader set of international energy efficiency standards.
decisions.qaRationale: schema.org/EnergyEfficiencyEnumeration is a controlled enumeration of rating values (A-G classes), whereas euelec:EnergyEfficiency is a composite data class aggregating efficiency class plus power-consumption metrics and EPREL registry info, so they are different kinds of concept rather than broader/narrower forms of the same one.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our class focuses specifically on EU Energy Label data and EPREL registry information, whereas the candidate covers all energy consumption details for products under various international regulations.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are classes modeling product energy-efficiency/labeling information, but ours is constrained to the EU Energy Label scheme (A-G classes, EPREL registry, Reg. 2019/2021) while schema.org's class spans international regimes including the US EPCA rule, making ours a narrower specialization.

decisions.bulkRationale: Our property specifies the EPREL registration number, a particular type of regulatory reference number; the candidate is a generic regulatory reference number.
decisions.qaRationale: EPREL is a regulatory product registry, so our EPREL registration number is a specific instance of the candidate's generic regulatory reference number, making ours narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: The upstream property lacks a definition, so we cannot confirm it matches the defined Power Consumption Off concept.
decisions.qaRationale: The identical local name and matching property type in our own core layer make this almost certainly the same 'power consumption when off but plugged in' concept, but the absent upstream definition prevents confirming full interchangeability, so CLOSE is the safest non-rejecting grade.

decisions.bulkRationale: Both properties provide a URL that directs to information about compliance with WEEE/EPR collection or take‑back schemes, though the candidate lacks a detailed definition.
decisions.qaRationale: Our term is a specific WEEE collection/take-back scheme URL whereas the candidate is a generic EPR-compliance URL property, and absent any candidate definition I cannot confirm they denote the same concept rather than merely overlapping naming.

decisions.bulkRationale: RoHS Compliance is a specific type of regulatory information, whereas Regulatory Information covers all government regulations for a trade item.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are classes about product regulatory data, and RoHS compliance is a specific instance of the broader 'applicable government regulations' concept, so our term is genuinely narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: RoHS Compliance is a specific type of certification, whereas Certification covers all kinds of official attestations.
decisions.qaRationale: RoHS Compliance is a specific regulatory compliance/attestation that fits as a narrower kind of schema.org Certification, though the match is approximate since compliance status differs subtly from a formal third-party certification.

decisions.bulkRationale: WEEE Compliance is a specific type of regulatory compliance for the WEEE Directive, whereas RegulatoryInformation covers all applicable government regulations.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are classes and WEEE compliance is a specific instance of regulatory information about a trade item, so ours is properly narrower than the generic gs1:RegulatoryInformation.

decisions.bulkRationale: Repair Criterion Type is a specific instance of the generic Criterion concept.
decisions.qaRationale: Our term is a domain-specific enumeration of the five French Repairability Index criteria, which is a proper specialization of the generic CCCEV Criterion class (both classes), so ours is narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: The RoHS Declaration URL is a specific type of referenced file, whereas referencedFileURL is a generic website URL.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are URL-valued properties and our RoHS declaration URL is a specific specialization of the generic gs1:referencedFileURL (referenced document URL), so ours is genuinely narrower.

decisions.bulkRationale: The EU Energy Label classes (A‑G) are a specific subset of the general energy efficiency concept represented by the upstream EnergyEfficiency class.
decisions.qaRationale: Both are classes in the energy-efficiency domain, and the EU A-G label grades are a specialized subset of the broader, undefined upstream EnergyEfficiency concept, so ours is narrower rather than interchangeable.

decisions.bulkRationale: The schema.org class represents general energy consumption details, while the OUR term specifies only EU Energy Label classes A‑G.
